author: fiddyschmitt
message:
Record Batch 8 (parallel bzip2 serving) in the performance plan

8a: multi-block groups decoded in parallel + buffered decoder input
(fixed a 2 MB/s gate bottleneck). 8b: bit-aligned block boundaries so
every ~900 KB block is its own entry (median 5.5 MB -> 900 KB),
decoded via in-memory bit-shift reconstruction. Validated byte-exact
on sda1 (3.7x single-thread floor) and MD5-gated through the mount
stack. Big-image cached number pending the next suite run.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Fable 5 <noreply@anthropic.com>
Claude-Session: https://claude.ai/code/session_01EzNgdgRVgqwy2gcCFfjPY8

author: fiddyschmitt
message:
bzip2: bit-aligned block boundaries lift the parallelism cap (Batch 8b)

The byte-aligned magic search only finds block boundaries that happen
to land on a byte (~1 in 8), merging the rest into coarse entries that
can only decode single-threaded - median 5.5 MB, with outliers into
the hundreds of MB. Scan every bit offset instead (rolling 64-bit
accumulator, 8 alignments checked per byte) so every ~900 KB block is
its own entry, and decode a block that starts mid-byte by
reconstructing a standalone byte-aligned .bz2 image in memory
(bzip2recover's bit-shift technique): the 4-byte file header followed
by the block's bits shifted onto the byte-4 boundary. A wrong
reconstruction fails the block CRC (loud), never silent garbage.

Mapping gains CompressedStart/EndBitInByte (0 for gzip and old
byte-aligned indexes, which then behave exactly as before). The cache
filename bumps to .bzip2_index_v2.json so existing indexes rebuild
once at the finer granularity.

Validated on sda1.img.bz2 with a fresh build: 458 entries (was 61),
median 900 KB, 397 starting mid-byte, byte-exact vs a full reference
decode across 100 random multi-entry reads plus every
entry-boundary/mid-entry/EOF read; sequential MD5 through the
CachingStream stack 35 MB/s (3.7x the single-thread floor, up from
1.8x in 8a). The few remaining >32 MB entries are single bzip2 blocks
whose RLE stage swallowed a large zero-run - inherent, and they decode
fast.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Fable 5 <noreply@anthropic.com>
Claude-Session: https://claude.ai/code/session_01EzNgdgRVgqwy2gcCFfjPY8

author: fiddyschmitt
message:
bzip2: parallel multi-block serving (Batch 8a)

Group consecutive index entries into ~32 MB spans; GetRecommendation
returns a whole group and Read decodes a group's entries concurrently,
each into its own slice of the caller's buffer (bzip2 blocks are
independently decodable; workers use independent SharedStream views).
Single-entry reads keep the inline path.

Also wrap the decoder's input in a BufferedStream: SharpCompress pulls
input in tiny reads, and each previously paid the SharedStream gate
(lock + FileStream reposition). That overhead dominated serving - a
576 MB sequential scan through the CachingStream stack ran at 2 MB/s
(0.2x a plain single-threaded decode); with buffering + parallel
groups it reaches 17 MB/s (1.8x) on the same giant-entry-dominated
file, byte-exact (md5 match vs reference, plus boundary/mid-entry/EOF
checks).

Parallel width is currently capped by index granularity: the
byte-aligned magic search merges ~6 real blocks per entry (median
5.5 MB) and leaves giant entries (up to 4.3 GB) that decode
single-threaded. Bit-aligned block finding lifts that cap - next
commit.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Fable 5 <noreply@anthropic.com>
Claude-Session: https://claude.ai/code/session_01EzNgdgRVgqwy2gcCFfjPY8

author: fiddyschmitt
message:
Drive images: persist the bzip2 block index (synthesize a cache)

The drive-image flow (CompressedImage) provides no partition cache,
and the cache synthesis added for gz/zstd excluded bzip2 - so
Bzip2Decompressor got a null index filename and Bzip2StreamSeekable
rebuilt the block index (a full parallel decode of the compressed
image) on every mount, never saving it. This is why the 2 TB bzip2
drive-image tests barely differed between fresh and cached suite runs
(~23 min each) while partition bzip2, whose index persists, collapsed
19.8 -> 3.2 min.

Add bzip2 to the synthesis so the index lands in the same whole-file
cache folder gz/zstd use. Validated on the 395 MB sda1.img.bz2 with a
scratch cache: run 1 builds and saves the .bzip2_index.json, run 2
loads it, identical listings.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Fable 5 <noreply@anthropic.com>
Claude-Session: https://claude.ai/code/session_01EzNgdgRVgqwy2gcCFfjPY8

author: fiddyschmitt
message:
Record investigations: bzip2 drive-image index never persisted; Sparse cleared

bzip2: CompressedImage passes no partition cache and the drive-image
cache synthesis covers only gz/zstd, so the block index is rebuilt on
every mount and never saved - the ~23 min "serving" cost is mostly
index construction. Fix identified (extend synthesis to BZip2).

Sparse.ExtractAndSparsifyFile: exercised path diffs 0 lines vs
baseline; isolated same-day A/B republish shows identical performance.
In-suite spread was E: cache state + contention. Closed, not a
regression.

author: fiddyschmitt
message:
Zstd: ZstdSeekable 0.3.0 - fill spans (ZSTZRAN3)

The package now records single-repeated-byte runs >= 1 MiB (zeros in
disk images, 0xFF in flash dumps) during the verified build and serves
reads inside them by filling the buffer directly - no window load, no
decoder, no compressed I/O. Resume points are not thinned inside runs,
so the MaxPointGap serving gate is unaffected.

One semantic to honour: ZstdIndexedStream.Read now also returns short
at fill-span edges (it stops decoding where a run begins so the next
read skips the decoder), which makes SeekableZstdStream's Read
fill-loop load-bearing rather than defensive - comment updated.

Existing ZSTZRAN2 indexes still load and serve; delete a .zsi and
re-index to gain fill spans (biggest win: the 2 TB drive image's null
tail).

Validated on a crafted 178 MB synthetic (test suite was live, so E:
untouched): 0x00/0xFF runs incl. a 40 MB run crossing point-span and
32 MB recommendation boundaries, a sub-threshold 512 KB run, an EOF
run - spans recorded byte-exact, sub-threshold ignored, every
decode<->fill edge byte-exact, CachingStream-stack MD5 match, reuse
from disk. ALL PASS.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Fable 5 <noreply@anthropic.com>
Claude-Session: https://claude.ai/code/session_01EzNgdgRVgqwy2gcCFfjPY8

author: fiddyschmitt
message:
Zstd: replace in-repo libZstd with the ZstdSeekable NuGet package

libZstd's v2 engine was upstreamed into the standalone ZstdSeekable
0.2.0 package (single-pass verified build, flat build RAM, incremental
.wip + resume, ZSTZRAN2 with ZSTZRAN1 loading, plus a
frame-start-only divergence fallback). Delete libZstd and reference
the package; keep a thin bridge in libClonezilla:

- SeekableZstdStream adapts ZstdIndexedStream to IReadSuggestor
  (32 MB-aligned sub-spans within a point span) and fill-loops Read
  for CachingStream's one-Read-per-recommendation contract; its
  recommendations return an empty range at/past EOF instead of
  throwing.
- ZstdDecompressor gates serving on index density (largest resume gap
  <= 4x TargetSpanBytes, else extraction fallback) so a degraded
  frame-start-only index on a huge single-frame stream never serves a
  mount worse than cache.train would.
- Serilog ILogger bridge + ~1 GB-interval build-progress reporter keep
  the house log format.

Index filename and format are unchanged, so existing
<partition>.zstd_index.zsi files stay valid. Validated by a harness
driving the production path (ZstdDecompressor + PartitionCache) on
sdb1: byte-exact random/boundary reads, sequential MD5 through the
CachingStream+suggestor stack, and reload-from-disk reuse.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Fable 5 <noreply@anthropic.com>
Claude-Session: https://claude.ai/code/session_01EzNgdgRVgqwy2gcCFfjPY8

author: fiddyschmitt
message:
zstd index build v2: single pass, verify-as-you-go, resumable (Batch 7 follow-up)

The v1 build verified after fully indexing: a second complete read+decode of the compressed source
(23.5 of sda2's 32.3 build minutes), plus drop-and-merge healing, plus every compressed window held
in RAM until the final save (~8 GB transient for the 2 TB drive image). And an interrupted build
lost everything.

v2 restructures the build into one sequential pass with two shadow decoders running alongside the
true decode:
- an INSURANCE shadow for the last confirmed point, covering its still-open span - so when its
  successor is confirmed, the point's entire serving span has been byte-verified against output that
  was in hand anyway (no re-read, no re-decode);
- a CANDIDATE shadow for the point under trial; divergence (normal, ~12% of boundaries) just re-arms
  at a later boundary while the insurance shadow keeps the open span covered - this also handles
  long zero-stretches end-to-end, which v1's bounded 4 MB trial could not;
- a diverging INSURANCE shadow (divergence deeper than a whole span; never observed on real data)
  aborts indexing and the caller falls back to extraction: never wrong data, at worst no index.

Sealed points are appended and flushed to the .wip file the moment they are confirmed (format v2
"ZSTZRAN2": inline compressed windows, gztool-style zeroed header counts until finalisation), so
build memory is ~10 MB flat and an interrupted build RESUMES: sealed points are kept, the build
fast-forwards from the last sealed frame-start point (keeping the verification truth chain rooted -
a zstd point cannot checkpoint full decoder state the way a gzip point can, which is why resume is
not gztool-cheap on single-frame sources), re-establishes the insurance shadow, and continues.

Measured (19.9 GB sda2, 45.4 GB uncompressed): 21.4 min vs v1's 32.3, producing the identical
677 points / 605.9 MB index. Validated byte-exact on sdb1 and sda1 including crafted-interruption
resume tests (truncated mid-record .wip -> resumed -> identical final index, all reads correct).
Shadows share the main thread; offloading them to a worker is a future lever if build CPU matters.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Fable 5 <noreply@anthropic.com>
Claude-Session: https://claude.ai/code/session_01EzNgdgRVgqwy2gcCFfjPY8

author: fiddyschmitt
message:
zstd index: report build and verification progress

The zstd index build was silent for its whole duration (tens of minutes on large images; an hour+
on the 2 TB drive image) between the opening and closing log lines, unlike every other long build
in the app (gz/bzip2 indexing and train extraction all report continuously). It now logs the house
format - Indexed <bytes>. (<percent>% through source file) - every 1 GB of decompressed output
during the build pass, and a Verified <n> of <total> resume points counter every 50 spans during
the verification pass.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Fable 5 <noreply@anthropic.com>
Claude-Session: https://claude.ai/code/session_01EzNgdgRVgqwy2gcCFfjPY8

author: fiddyschmitt
message:
Streams: replace IndependentStream with SharedStream.CreateView() (pattern from ZstdSeekable)

The old sharing pattern required every call site to know both the shared lock object and the wrapper
class, and to thread them through correctly (8 sites did this by hand). Back-port the standalone
ZstdSeekable package's approach: a SharedStream owns the base stream and a private gate, and mints
independent read cursors via CreateView() - callers never see the lock, the view class, or the
sharing rules; the contract is correct by construction.

- libCommon: new SharedStream (private nested view). The view locks EVERY touch of the base stream
  on the gate - including Length - which resolves plan item P9 exactly as prescribed: the redundant
  Stream.Synchronized layer IndependentStream carried (kept only for Length/Position atomicity) is
  gone, with the atomicity concern addressed properly. Position is a view-local field.
- SubStream: parameter retyped from IndependentStream to Stream (it only ever used Stream members;
  the doc now states the independent-cursor contract).
- Converted all 8 construction sites: Bzip2StreamSeekable (read path + parallel index build),
  GZipStreamSeekable, ZstdStreamSeekable, ZstdSeekableIndex.VerifyAndHeal, RawDriveImage,
  TrainDecompressor, MountedPartitionImage and Program.ListContents (the extractor stream factories
  are now just sharedPartitionStream.CreateView).
- ZstdStreamSeekable additionally gains a public CreateView() mirroring the package: an independent
  cursor sharing the source, gate, index and mappings.
- IndependentStream deleted.

Remaining Stream.Synchronized uses reviewed and deliberately kept: SynchronisedExtractor (serialises
one shared extracted stream across Dokan handles - a different job) and CompressedImage/ImageFile
(guard mixed direct-sequential + shared access phases on drive-image streams).

Validated: full solution build; gz zran harness (16/16 index points vs gztool -ll, stored-block
crossing reads, block spans/edges/300 random reads byte-exact) and zstd harness (6 verified points,
block starts/crossings, 200 random + 50 reloaded reads, sequential full-read MD5) both ALL PASS.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Fable 5 <noreply@anthropic.com>
Claude-Session: https://claude.ai/code/session_01EzNgdgRVgqwy2gcCFfjPY8

author: fiddyschmitt
message:
libZstd: rename assembly to libZstdSeekable (case-insensitive publish collision with native libzstd.dll)

The single-file publish flattens ZstdNet's native libzstd.dll into the same output directory as our
managed assemblies; on case-insensitive Windows paths, libZstd.dll collides with it and publish
fails with NETSDK1152 (surfaced by Visual Studio only as its generic WebTools error). Project
folder and namespaces remain libZstd; only the output assembly is renamed.

Verified: dotnet publish with the FolderProfile now succeeds and the deployed exe runs.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Fable 5 <noreply@anthropic.com>
Claude-Session: https://claude.ai/code/session_01EzNgdgRVgqwy2gcCFfjPY8

author: fiddyschmitt
message:
Drive images: synthesize a partition cache so gz/zstd use their indexes (not extraction)

The bare drive-image flow (CompressedImage - e.g. sda.img.gz / sda.img.zst) passed no partition
cache to DecompressorSelector, so GzDecompressor and ZstdDecompressor - which need a cache object to
know where to keep their index files - returned no seekable stream and ALWAYS fell back to
extracting the whole decompressed image to cache.train. The gz drive-image listing has been a 2 TB
extraction all along; zstd would have kept extracting despite Batch 7.

DecompressorSelector now synthesizes a PartitionCache whenever none was provided and the format has
index support (gz, zstd), rooted in the same whole-file hash folder the extraction cache uses (the
hash computation is extracted into GetWholeFileCacheFolder(), byte-identical key, so existing cache
folders remain valid and index files land beside cache.train).

Fixed in the same change: the hash helper reads 50 MB of decompressed content and used to leave the
compressed stream positioned mid-file; the gzip index build pipes the stream to gztool from its
CURRENT position, so it received a headless stream and produced a corrupt index. The helper now
seeks back to 0. (Diagnosing this also established that stdin-built gztool indexes are format v1 -
gzipindX with line numbers - which GztoolIndex already parses.)

Smoke-tested end-to-end on sda1.img.gz through the real binary: gztool index built (137 access
points at the 4 MiB span), parsed for in-process reads, listing correct, and the index is reused on
a second run. The zstd drive path shares the identical wiring.

See PERFORMANCE_PLAN.md (Batch 7 follow-up).

Co-Authored-By: Claude Fable 5 <noreply@anthropic.com>
Claude-Session: https://claude.ai/code/session_01EzNgdgRVgqwy2gcCFfjPY8

author: fiddyschmitt
message:
zstd: in-memory random access via verified resume points (Batch 7)

Large zstd partitions previously fell back to extracting the whole decompressed stream to an
on-disk cache.train (Clonezilla's -z9 output is a single standard zstd frame, ~2 MB window - not the
seekable format). New libZstd gives standard zstd streams the same in-memory seekable treatment gz
(gztool index) and bzip2 (block index) already have.

The zstd-specific hard part: a block can depend on decoder state beyond the window (repeat offsets,
carried entropy tables), so unlike gzip an arbitrary block boundary is NOT a safe resume point.
Measured on real Clonezilla output: ~88% of boundaries resume correctly via a synthetic frame header
+ ZSTD_DCtx_refPrefix(window); the rest diverge - sometimes silently and megabytes late (long
RLE/zero runs neither use nor update repeat-offset state, hiding stale state from any bounded
lookahead; observed on real data).

Correctness is therefore baked in empirically:
- ZstdSeekableIndex builds by one sequential block-fed decode (ZstdSharp), arming a candidate point
  every ~64 MB and trial-decoding 4 MB through a shadow decoder before acceptance.
- Every span is then verified end-to-end (piecewise MD5 vs the true decode at every candidate
  boundary; contiguous per-worker slices to stay disk-friendly). An unsound point is dropped and its
  predecessor re-verified over the merged span - healing terminates because a frame-start resume IS
  the true decode. If verification cannot converge, no index is produced and DecompressorSelector
  falls back to extraction exactly as before.
- ZstdStreamSeekable clamps every read to its verified span (only output within a span is proven;
  decoder state at its end is not) and recommends 32 MB sub-spans so the CachingStream above stays
  within Batch 6 (E)'s pooled-buffer sizes. Windows live zstd-compressed in the .zsi index, loaded
  lazily.

ZstdNet remains the sequential/train decoder; ZstdSharp (managed zstd port) is used only where the
advanced API is required. Wired via IPartitionCache.GetZstdIndexFilename().

Validated byte-exact against in-RAM ground truth on real images (sdb1 345 MB: block starts/edges/
cross-chunk/200 random reads/reload/sequential-MD5 all pass; sda1 417 MB nearly-incompressible: all
pass); 19.9 GB sda2 pass-1 at scale produced 677 candidates cleanly. Full suite run pending.

See PERFORMANCE_PLAN.md Batch 7.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Fable 5 <noreply@anthropic.com>
Claude-Session: https://claude.ai/code/session_01EzNgdgRVgqwy2gcCFfjPY8

author: fiddyschmitt
message:
Plan: pagefile-skip needs a product decision, not a silent default

Co-Authored-By: Claude Fable 5 <noreply@anthropic.com>
Claude-Session: https://claude.ai/code/session_01EzNgdgRVgqwy2gcCFfjPY8

author: fiddyschmitt
message:
Cache: pool the segment buffers instead of allocating per miss (Batch 6 E)

Every CachingStream cache miss allocated a fresh multi-MB byte[] (the block-sized read
recommendation) and dropped it to the GC on eviction - the main source of the LOH churn measured
during full-copy runs (gcFrag spiking to ~2 GB, violent gen2 saw-toothing). Segments now rent from
Buffers.BufferPool (already sized for 50 MB arrays) and return on eviction, on Close(), and on the
serve-without-caching path.

Rented arrays can be larger than requested, so CacheEntry.Content may exceed the entry's Length;
every consumer is bounded by Start/End (verified: no consumers outside CachingStream itself). The
short-read Array.Resize copy is gone for the same reason. Entries supplied via precapturedCache are
marked unpooled and never returned.

See PERFORMANCE_PLAN.md Batch 6 (E). Complements (B): denser checkpoints cut how much is decoded
and discarded per cold seek; pooling cuts the allocation cost of what remains.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Fable 5 <noreply@anthropic.com>
Claude-Session: https://claude.ai/code/session_01EzNgdgRVgqwy2gcCFfjPY8

author: fiddyschmitt
message:
gz: denser index access points, 4 MiB span instead of 10 (Batch 6 B)

A cold read costs on average half a span of decode-and-discard from the nearest access point, so
point density directly multiplies scattered-read throughput - the fragmented-file pathology
(files whose NTFS clusters scatter across the partition read at 0.1-0.6 MB/s because every
cluster-run pays the discard). 4 MiB spans cut the average discard from ~5 MB to ~2 MB per cold
seek (~2.5x less decode work), which compounds with the in-process zran reads (I0).

Cost: a ~2.5x bigger on-disk index (e.g. 68 MB -> ~180 MB for a 45 GB partition), built once per
image. Point windows are loaded lazily by file offset, so resident memory is unaffected by density.
Existing cached indexes keep their original 10 MiB density and remain fully valid.

Verified with the zranproto harness: a -s 4 index (16 points vs 7) parses, matches gztool -ll
16/16, and all byte-exactness checks pass on synthetic and production data.

See PERFORMANCE_PLAN.md Batch 6 (B).

Co-Authored-By: Claude Fable 5 <noreply@anthropic.com>
Claude-Session: https://claude.ai/code/session_01EzNgdgRVgqwy2gcCFfjPY8

author: fiddyschmitt
message:
Cache: share the RAM budget across all partitions (Batch 6 A)

The layer-4 CachingStream was budgeted at 1/4 of system RAM PER PARTITION, so a 3-partition mount
could commit 3/4 of RAM to decompression caches - the measured root cause of the memory exhaustion
during full-copy runs (free RAM fell to ~400 MB, the machine paged itself unusable, and reads
started failing with 0x800705AA).

LimitByRAMUsage instances now split their declared budget evenly: a process-wide count of live
instances divides each one's cap (CacheLimitValue / liveCount). All callers pass the same 1/4-RAM
value, so the TOTAL cache commitment stays <= 1/4 RAM regardless of how many partitions are mounted.
No cross-instance locking - each instance still evicts only from its own LRU list; an instance that
is never Close()d keeps its slot reserved, which errs towards using less memory, never more.

See PERFORMANCE_PLAN.md Batch 6 (A).

Co-Authored-By: Claude Fable 5 <noreply@anthropic.com>
Claude-Session: https://claude.ai/code/session_01EzNgdgRVgqwy2gcCFfjPY8

author: fiddyschmitt
message:
gz: decode reads in-process (zran) instead of spawning gztool per read (I0)

Every cache-miss read of a gz-backed stream used to spawn gztool.exe, feed it the compressed span
over stdin and read the output back over stdout - a large per-read cost (a dominant share of the
1-4 MB/s mount baseline) and a reliability hazard: a failed spawn under memory pressure hard-failed
the read (the jboss xsd copy failure and the ext4_lvm suite hang). gztool remains the index BUILDER;
reads now decode in-process:

- GztoolIndex parses gztool's binary .gzi directly (v0/v1, big-endian, format from gztool 1.8.2's
  serialize_index_to_file). Point windows stay on disk and load lazily - TB-scale images have 100k+
  points. This also replaces the "gztool -ll" text-scrape for the block mappings.
- Vendored SharpZipLib v1.4.2 inflater (MIT; libGZip/Vendored/SharpZipLib/README.md has provenance)
  with two additions: StreamManipulator.PrimeBits (zlib inflatePrime equivalent) and
  Inflater.PrimeForResume (raw-mode inflateSetDictionary + prime). No existing BCL/NuGet inflater
  can resume mid-stream: DeflateStream exposes neither primitive (and a bit-shifted input stream is
  not a valid substitute - stored blocks re-align to the original byte grid), and ZLibDotNet 0.1.1
  mis-decodes real DEFLATE streams mid-stream.
- ZranInflate pumps from the access point (prime split bits, preload <=32 KB window) straight into
  the caller's buffer.
- GZipStreamSeekable serves reads in-process and falls back to the old gztool-subprocess path
  automatically (unparseable index -> whole stream; per-read anomaly such as a multi-member gzip
  member ending inside a span -> that read). The brittle bytesRead != bytesToRead hard-fail is out
  of the read path.

Verified byte-exact against ground truth: synthetic 64 MB gz (stored-block regions included) -
7/7 parsed points match gztool -ll, all block spans / point edges / 300 random reads pass through
GZipStreamSeekable; production sda2.ntfs-ptcl-img.gz index (4,317 points, 45.4 GB uncompressed,
bits 0-7 all represented) - 12 random direct-vs-decode-through cross-checks byte-identical;
~54 MB/s decompressed per cold 10 MB span. Full suite run pending.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Fable 5 <noreply@anthropic.com>
Claude-Session: https://claude.ai/code/session_01EzNgdgRVgqwy2gcCFfjPY8

author: fiddyschmitt
message:
SharpCompress: rebuild the patched package from the 0.49.1 TAG (fixes the xz perf regression)

The 06-23 local package (0.49.1-localbzip2patch) was accidentally built from the fork's default
branch = upstream MASTER (0.49.1 + ~3.5 weeks of dev commits), one of which regressed XZ decode
~1.7x in sparse/high-compression regions. Dense-region decode is unaffected, which is what let a
head-only benchmark wrongly exonerate the package; the regression cost ~60-70 min on the 10h suite's
Xz drive-image listing (a ~2 TB mostly-null decode).

Null-region micro-bench (8 GB-of-zeros xz, MB/s): SharpCompress 0.37 = 275; official 0.49.1 = 283;
old master-based pack = 159; NEW tag-based pack = 283. The new 0.49.1-localbzip2patch2 is the
official 0.49.1 source plus only the (upstream-merged) bzip2 tolerateTruncatedStream patch -
adamhathcock/sharpcompress#1358, merged 2026-06-23, not yet in any release.

Full-suite validation: Xz listing 248-265 -> 192.8 min, matching the pre-migration baseline binary
(194) on the same machine; 74/74 green incl. all bzip2 tests.

Once upstream ships a release containing PR #1358: switch both PackageReferences to the official
version and delete nuget.config + the local-nuget feed.

PERFORMANCE_PLAN.md documents the full regression investigation (Experiments 1-4 + resolution).

Co-Authored-By: Claude Fable 5 <noreply@anthropic.com>
Claude-Session: https://claude.ai/code/session_01EzNgdgRVgqwy2gcCFfjPY8

author: fiddyschmitt
message:
VFS: verify the mounted volume is ours before using it (I1 crash fix)

A 4h ListContents run died with an unhandled FileNotFoundException opening a virtual file on its own
internal mount: rclone (in another session) owned the drive letter, and our mount logic - which only
waits for the letter to APPEAR - silently adopted the foreign volume, on which our paths don't exist.
A letter squatted by another session can also look free to the GetLogicalDrives scan.

Each mount attempt now plants a uniquely-named UnlistedFolder sentinel in a fresh RootFolder and,
once the letter appears, probes it THROUGH the mounted letter (briefly retried while the mount
settles). Only our own live VFS instance can answer, so a squatter (rclone/subst/network mapping) is
detected deterministically. On mismatch the attempt is torn down and:
- an auto-chosen letter falls back to the next free one, excluding letters already tried (up to 3
  attempts, then Log.Fatal);
- a user-chosen letter (-m) fails loudly naming the squatting volume - no silent substitution.

Also:
- WaitForFolderToExist gained a timeout (30s here): a failed Dokan mount previously left the app
  spinning forever on while(true).
- The mount verbs log and open Explorer at the ACTUAL mounted letter, which can differ from the
  tentative one after a fallback.

Validated by two full 74/74 suite runs; root cause confirmed by a rclone-off run on the unfixed
binary (crash did not recur). See PERFORMANCE_PLAN.md Investigation I1.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Fable 5 <noreply@anthropic.com>
Claude-Session: https://claude.ai/code/session_01EzNgdgRVgqwy2gcCFfjPY8

author: fiddyschmitt
message:
Dokan: shared timeout watchdog instead of a per-read Timer (D6 throughput fix)

The D6 watchdog allocated, scheduled, and disposed a System.Threading.Timer on every ReadFile - even
the millions of tiny fast metadata reads a drive-image tree-walk issues. Each took the global
timer-queue lock; a bisect attributed ~14 min of the full-suite regression to this churn (it also
inflated unrelated per-read work via GC/lock pressure).

Replace it with one free-running static Timer (5s) that walks a ConcurrentDictionary of in-flight
reads and extends each still-running one via TryResetTimeout, up to the 10-min runaway cap.
StartTimeoutWatchdog now just inserts into that dictionary and returns a readonly struct whose
Dispose() removes it, so `using var` allocates nothing and the fast path pays only a dictionary
insert/remove. A fast read completes between ticks and is never touched; only genuinely-slow reads
(still alive at a 5s boundary) get extended - same 0x800705AA protection D6 gave, without the churn.

Validated by a full 74/74 suite run (693 min, down from 759): gz and zst large-drive-image listings
back to baseline, slow-read tests (ext4_lvm, dd, Ubuntu FS) still pass. The remaining xz regression is
a separate cause (D3 per-read item reopen) - see PERFORMANCE_PLAN.md.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.8 <noreply@anthropic.com>
Claude-Session: https://claude.ai/code/session_01EzNgdgRVgqwy2gcCFfjPY8

author: Fidel
message:
Dokan: extend the deadline for genuinely-slow reads (D6)

A full Explorer copy of a gz image hit 0x800705AA on heavily-fragmented files (logs / Temp / WU
download blobs). Those read at ~0.1-0.6 MB/s - their clusters scatter across the partition, so reads
seek all over the gz stream and re-decompress past the cache - so a few-MB file takes 25-70s, exceeding
the 20s Dokan timeout; the driver then abandons the IRP (surfaces as 0x800705AA, can unmount).

Add a watchdog around ReadFile (covers the mmap branch too): while a read runs, a timer extends the
operation's deadline every 10s via IDokanFileInfo.TryResetTimeout(20s), capped at 10 minutes so a true
hang still fails. A fast read disposes the timer before its first tick, so the common path pays nothing.
This is the legitimate TryResetTimeout case D5 set aside (now justified by measured slow reads).

Read throughput itself is a separate follow-up (see PERFORMANCE_PLAN.md "Future investigation - mount
read throughput"). See CODE_REVIEW_PLAN.md D6.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.8 <noreply@anthropic.com>
Claude-Session: https://claude.ai/code/session_01EzNgdgRVgqwy2gcCFfjPY8

author: Fidel
message:
Dokan: set an explicit operation timeout (D5)

Make the per-operation timeout explicit instead of relying on the driver default (15s): set
options.TimeOut = 20s. After D1-D4 every callback is bounded (a single ReadFile decompresses at most
one buffer; the slow $MFT parse happens at mount time, outside callbacks), so this is purely a safety
net before Dokan abandons an IRP - which can surface to the app as 0x800705AA and, at the threshold,
unmount the volume.

SingleThread is left at its default (false) so Dokan keeps dispatching callbacks on multiple threads;
documented with a comment.

Deliberately no TryResetTimeout: per the Dokan docs it is for callbacks expected to outlast the
timeout (via a per-operation watchdog timer, e.g. remote/cold-storage fetches). Our reads are bounded
well under 20s, and a naive one-shot reset would only delay - and thereby mask - a genuine hang.

See CODE_REVIEW_PLAN.md D5.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.8 <noreply@anthropic.com>
Claude-Session: https://claude.ai/code/session_01EzNgdgRVgqwy2gcCFfjPY8

author: Fidel
message:
Dokan: reuse one stream for the memory-mapped read path (D4)

The mmap (paging) branch of ReadFile opened a fresh stream and disposed it on every page fault (these
reads carry no handle context, so unlike normal reads they couldn't stash a per-handle stream). After
D3 that was no longer a full worker checkout, but it still churned a stream per fault.

- FileEntry.ReadForMemoryMap keeps one reusable stream per file for paging reads:
  - per-handle entries (CreatesNewStreamPerCall, e.g. the native extractor): lazily creates and caches
    an independent stream, serialised by its own lock. Cheap to keep - a PooledNativeItemStream holds no
    worker (borrows one only per read).
  - shared-stream entries (!CreatesNewStreamPerCall): uses the shared stream under FileEntry.ReadLock,
    the same lock normal reads use, so the two stay mutually exclusive on that single stream.
- DokanVFS mmap branch is now just bytesRead = file.ReadForMemoryMap(...).

See CODE_REVIEW_PLAN.md D4.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.8 <noreply@anthropic.com>
Claude-Session: https://claude.ai/code/session_01EzNgdgRVgqwy2gcCFfjPY8

author: Fidel
message:
Dokan: decouple open file handles from native workers (D3)

CreateFile -> GetStream -> Extract used to checkout a pool worker (blocking until one was free) and hold
it for the open handle's whole lifetime. So more than poolSize (4) files open for read on one partition
blocked CreateFile until a handle closed -> Dokan timeout -> 0x800705AA (thumbnails, preview pane,
robocopy /MT, AV scans).

Borrow a worker per Read instead of per open handle:

- New PooledNativeItemStream (returned by Extract) holds no worker - just (pool, index, length,
  position). Seek/Position are logical. Each Read borrows a worker, opens the item on it, seeks to
  position, fills the buffer under that single borrow, then returns the worker. Workers share the
  partition's decompression cache, so the re-seek is cheap.
- NativeExtractorPool.Extract returns the lightweight stream (no Take); added internal
  TakeWorker/ReturnWorker (ReturnWorker tolerates unmount-teardown races).
- SevenZipExtractorUsingNative.OpenItemStream: onClosed is now optional.

Net: open handles are unbounded (CreateFile never blocks); the pool size now caps concurrent reads, not
open files. One ReadFile maps to one worker borrow. See CODE_REVIEW_PLAN.md D3.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.8 <noreply@anthropic.com>
Claude-Session: https://claude.ai/code/session_01EzNgdgRVgqwy2gcCFfjPY8

author: Fidel
message:
Dokan: scope the read lock to stream ownership (D2)

ReadFile locked FileEntry.ReadLock (shared by every handle of a file) for the whole seek+decompress,
so two handles reading the same file serialized even though each has its own stream. Match the lock to
stream ownership:

- FileEntryStream gains a per-handle ReadLock.
- Normal read, per-handle stream (CreatesNewStreamPerCall): lock the per-handle wrapper - same-handle
  reads stay serialized, but different handles to the same file now run in parallel.
- Normal read, shared stream (!CreatesNewStreamPerCall, e.g. mount-as-image-files): still locks
  FileEntry.ReadLock (one stream, must serialize) - unchanged.
- Mmap read, fresh per-call stream: no lock (created for one read, shared with nobody); shared-stream
  mmap reads still take FileEntry.ReadLock.
- Removed two stale commented-out experimental blocks in the old lock body.

Safe because CreatesNewStreamPerCall == true means GetStream() hands out a distinct stream per handle,
so cross-handle locking was never needed for those. See CODE_REVIEW_PLAN.md D2.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.8 <noreply@anthropic.com>
Claude-Session: https://claude.ai/code/session_01EzNgdgRVgqwy2gcCFfjPY8

author: Fidel
message:
Dokan: never let exceptions escape callbacks (D1)

An unhandled exception in a Dokan callback becomes a generic driver failure that surfaces to the
calling app as 0x800705AA (ERROR_NO_SYSTEM_RESOURCES). Make the callbacks that traverse user paths or
open streams return an NtStatus instead of throwing:

- ReadFile: wrap the body; on failure log + return DokanResult.Error (bytesRead=0). The two throws
  become FileNotFound (mmap path, missing entry) and Unsuccessful (missing info.Context).
- CreateFile: a failing GetStream() now logs + returns DokanResult.Error (was an uncaught throw).
- GetFileInformation: an unresolved path returns FileNotFound (was a throw).
- FindFiles / FindFilesWithPattern: wrapped; on failure log + return an empty list + DokanResult.Error.

Trivial callbacks (constants/NotImplemented), GetDiskFreeSpace (stable in-memory snapshot), and
FlushFileBuffers (already guarded) can't throw and are unchanged. See CODE_REVIEW_PLAN.md D1.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.8 <noreply@anthropic.com>
Claude-Session: https://claude.ai/code/session_01EzNgdgRVgqwy2gcCFfjPY8
